root.narration: Bottom of the sixth, your team trails by one. Runner on second, one out. You're at the plate -- one of the better hitters in the lineup, batting .350. A single to the outfield probably scores the tying run. The pitcher has been throwing mostly fastballs on the outer half today. You're in a 1-0 count -- a hitter's count. How do you approach this at-bat?

choices.text: Look to hit the ball the other way. The pitcher has been living on the outer half, so I'll go with the pitch and drive it to the opposite field gap. A hard single or double that way scores the runner easily.
Sit on the inside fastball and try to pull it for a home run! One swing can put us ahead!
Just try to hit the ball hard up the middle. Don't try to do too much -- let the ball dictate where it goes.
Sacrifice bunt to move the runner to third. Then a sac fly, passed ball, or anything can score him.

outcome-oppo-great.narration: The pitcher throws another fastball on the outer half -- right into your plan. You stay patient, let the ball travel deep into the zone, extend your hands, and drive it to the opposite field gap! The ball shoots past the diving outfielder and rolls to the wall! Your runner on second was already reading the ball off the bat -- he rounds third and scores EASILY! You pull into second with a stand-up double! TIE GAME! The opposing coach can't believe it -- you went with the pitch perfectly and turned their game plan against them!

outcome.headline: RBI Double! Opposite Field Gap!
outcome.explanation: This is elite situational hitting. Here's why going opposite field was the perfect approach: 1) The pitcher was pitching you AWAY, so the ball was already on the outer half. Going with it is easier than fighting it. 2) The opposite field gap is often the biggest gap on the field because outfielders tend to shade toward the pull side. 3) Letting the ball travel deeper into the zone gives you a better look at the pitch, leading to harder, more consistent contact. 4) A runner on second scores on almost any ball hit to the outfield gap. You didn't need a home run -- you needed a gap hit, and you got it by matching your approach to the situation.
outcome.whatToRemember: When the pitcher is living on the outer half, go WITH the pitch to the opposite field. Let the ball travel deep, extend your hands, and drive it. Don't fight the pitch -- use it.

outcome-pull-bad.narration: You sit dead-red looking to pull. The pitcher throws a fastball on the outer half -- his bread and butter. You try to pull it, but because the pitch is away, you have to commit early and roll your hands over. The result? A weak grounder to the shortstop. The shortstop fields it and looks the runner back to second, then throws to first for the out. Two outs, runner still on second. You tried to pull an outside pitch and produced the weakest possible contact.

outcome.headline: Weak Grounder! Can't Pull an Outside Pitch!
outcome.explanation: Trying to pull an outside pitch is one of the most common mistakes hitters make. When the ball is on the outer half and you try to pull it, your bat has to travel a longer path to reach the ball, and you end up rolling over on top of it. The result is almost always a weak grounder to the pull side. The physics don't work: outside pitches are DESIGNED to be hit the other way. Going with the pitch would have produced hard contact to the gap. Fighting the pitch produced a routine grounder. In situational hitting, your job isn't to hit a home run -- it's to hit the ball hard somewhere. Match your swing to the pitch location.
outcome.whatToRemember: Don't try to pull outside pitches. Outside pitches go the other way, inside pitches get pulled. Match your swing to the pitch location for the hardest contact.

outcome-middle-good.narration: You go up looking to hit the ball hard up the middle -- a solid, no-frills approach. The fastball comes in on the outer half and you put a good swing on it. Line drive back through the box! The pitcher ducks, the ball skips through the middle into center field! The runner on second rounds third and scores! TIE GAME! A hard single up the middle gets the job done!

outcome.headline: RBI Single! Hard Line Drive Up the Middle!
outcome.explanation: The 'hit it hard up the middle' approach is never wrong -- it's the safest, most consistent approach a hitter can have. You made hard contact, scored the run, and got on base. Great result. The only reason it's not a perfect approach here is that the pitcher was pitching you AWAY. Going with the outside pitch to the opposite field would have produced a double to the gap instead of a single up the middle. But in situational hitting, a hit is a hit. The runner scored, which is all that matters. 'Hit it hard somewhere' is always a good plan.
outcome.whatToRemember: Hitting the ball hard up the middle is always a solid approach. But when you can read the pitcher's pattern (outer half all day), adjusting to go the other way can produce even better results.

outcome-bunt-okay.narration: You square around to bunt. The pitcher sees it and throws a high fastball -- tough to bunt. You get it down, but it rolls too hard toward the third baseman. He charges, fields it, and checks the runner at third -- the runner holds. Throw to first, you're out. Runner on third, two outs. You moved the runner up, but you gave away an out from your .350 hitter. Now you need someone ELSE to get the hit.

outcome.headline: Runner to Third, But You Gave Up Your At-Bat.
outcome.explanation: Bunting to move a runner from second to third with one out isn't a terrible play, but it's a waste of a good hitter. You're batting .350 -- that means you get a hit about one out of every three at-bats. A .350 hitter should be SWINGING, not bunting. The sacrifice bunt makes more sense with a weak hitter at the plate. With you, the expected value of swinging is much higher than bunting. Also, with one out already, giving away another out puts enormous pressure on the next hitter. Going from one out to two outs is a BIG drop in run expectancy. Use your bat -- that's your weapon.
outcome.whatToRemember: Don't sacrifice bunt with a good hitter. The bat is too valuable. Sacrifice bunts are for weak hitters or very specific late-game situations. A .350 hitter should be swinging.
